channel.title: Parse-Error on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Parse-Error on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] Bash Syntax Error Near Unexpected Token
item.description:
Bash Syntax Error Near Unexpected Token

This error occurs when Bash encounters a character or token it doesn’t expect while parsing a command or script.

What This Error Means

Bash follows strict syntax rules for command structure. When it encounters something out of place, it throws a parse error before execution begins.

Common Causes

  • Unclosed quotes
  • Misplaced parentheses or brackets
  • Special characters not escaped
  • Windows line endings (CRLF)

How to Fix

Fix 1: Check and close all quotes

# Wrong
echo "Hello world

# Right
echo "Hello world"

Fix 2: Escape special characters

# Wrong
echo Hello;World

# Right
echo "Hello;World"
echo Hello\;World

Fix 3: Convert Windows line endings

sed -i 's/\r$//' script.sh

Fix 4: Check parenthesis usage

# Wrong
var = (1 2 3)

# Right
var=(1 2 3)

Examples

$ echo "Hello
bash: syntax error near unexpected token `newline'

$ echo Hello )world
bash: syntax error near unexpected token `)'

Related Errors

  • Unmatched Quote - unexpected end of file due to unclosed quotes
  • Here Document Error - syntax issues with heredoc syntax

item.title: [Solution] Bash Syntax Error Near Unexpected Token
item.description:
Bash Syntax Error Near Unexpected Token Fix

This error occurs when Bash encounters a character or token it doesn’t expect while parsing a command or script.

Description

Bash follows strict syntax rules for command structure. When it encounters something out of place — like an unclosed quote, misplaced parenthesis, or unexpected character — it throws a parse error before execution even begins.

Common Causes

  • Unclosed quotes — a missing closing " or ' makes Bash think the next token is part of the string.
  • Misplaced parentheses or brackets — using ( or ) in the wrong context.
  • Special characters not escaped — spaces, semicolons, or other metacharacters used literally without quoting.
  • Windows line endings (CRLF) — carriage return characters confuse the parser.

How to Fix

Fix 1: Check and close all quotes

# Wrong
echo "Hello world

# Right
echo "Hello world"

Fix 2: Escape special characters

# Wrong
echo Hello;World

# Right
echo "Hello;World"
# Or escape it
echo Hello\;World

Fix 3: Convert Windows line endings

sed -i 's/\r$//' script.sh

Fix 4: Check parenthesis usage

# Wrong — Bash thinks this is a subshell
var = (1 2 3)

# Right — use proper array syntax
var=(1 2 3)

Examples

$ echo "Hello
bash: syntax error near unexpected token `newline'

$ echo Hello )world
bash: syntax error near unexpected token `)'

$ ./script.sh
# script.sh has Windows line endings
bash: ./script.sh: line 1: $'\r': command not found

Related Errors

  • Unmatched Quote — unexpected end of file due to unclosed quotes.
  • Here Document Error — syntax issues with heredoc syntax.

item.title: [Solution] Bash Unexpected End of File (Unmatched Quote)
item.description:
Bash Unexpected End of File (Unmatched Quote) Fix

This error occurs when Bash reaches the end of input while still inside an unclosed quote, command substitution, or other delimited construct.

Description

Bash tracks open quotes, parentheses, braces, and command substitutions. If input ends before these are closed, Bash reports an unexpected end of file. The most common cause is a missing closing quote.

Common Causes

  • Unclosed double or single quote — the opening " or ' has no matching closer.
  • Missing closing bracket — $(command without ).
  • Incomplete here-document — missing EOF delimiter.
  • Multi-line string without proper continuation — forgetting the closing delimiter.

How to Fix

Fix 1: Find and close unclosed quotes

# Wrong
msg="Hello world

# Right
msg="Hello world"

Fix 2: Close command substitutions

# Wrong
result=$(echo "test"

# Right
result=$(echo "test")

Fix 3: Use syntax highlighting to spot mismatches

Most editors highlight matching quotes and brackets. Enable it to visually track unclosed delimiters.

item.title: [Solution] nlohmann/json Parse Error at Offset Fix
item.description:
nlohmann/json Parse Error at Offset Fix

Fix nlohmann/json parse errors at offset. Handle unexpected tokens, trailing characters, and encoding issues.

What This Error Means

nlohmann/json throws json::parse_error with the exact byte offset:

[json.exception.parse_error.101] parse error at line 2, column 5: syntax error while parsing value - unexpected ';'

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Trailing comma
auto j = json::parse(R"({"a": 1,})");

// Cause 2: Trailing characters after JSON
auto j = json::parse(R"({"a": 1} some text)");

// Cause 3: Unescaped control characters
auto j = json::parse(R"({"key": "line1\nline2"})"); // Wrong

// Cause 4: Single quotes instead of double quotes
auto j = json::parse("{'key': 'value'}");

// Cause 5: Comments in JSON (not standard)
auto j = json::parse(R"({"a": 1 /* comment */})");

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use try-catch for error handling

#include <nlohmann/json.hpp>
#include <iostream>

using json = nlohmann::json;

json safe_parse(const std::string& input) {
 try {
 return json::parse(input);
 } catch (const json::parse_error& e) {
 std::cerr << "Parse error at byte " << e.byte
 << ": " << e.what() << std::endl;
 return json(); // Return null JSON
 }
}

Fix 2: Accept trailing commas with option

#include <nlohmann/json.hpp>

json parse_relaxed(const std::string& input) {
 return json::parse(input, nullptr, false); // Allow exceptions to be ignored
}

Fix 3: Use json::accept to validate first

#include <nlohmann/json.hpp>

bool is_valid_json(const std::string& input) {
 return json::accept(input);
}

Examples

#include <nlohmann/json.hpp>
#include <iostream>

using json = nlohmann::json;

void process_json(const std::string& raw) {
 try {
 json data = json::parse(raw);

 if (data.contains("name")) {
 std::string name = data["name"].get<std::string>();
 std::cout << "Name: " << name << std::endl;
 }

 if (data.contains("items") && data["items"].is_array()) {
 for (const auto& item : data["items"]) {
 std::cout << "Item: " << item.dump() << std::endl;
 }
 }
 } catch (const json::parse_error& e) {
 std::cerr << "Parse error at offset " << e.byte << ": " << e.what() << std::endl;
 } catch (const json::type_error& e) {
 std::cerr << "Type error: " << e.what() << std::endl;
 }
}

int main() {
 process_json(R"({"name": "test", "items": [1, 2, 3]})");
 return 0;
}

Related Errors

  • Nlohmann JSON Error — nlohmann JSON error
  • Boost JSON Error — Boost JSON error
  • RapidJSON Error — RapidJSON error

item.title: [Solution] Ruby Nokogiri XML Parse Error Fix
item.description:
What This Error Means

A Nokogiri XML parse error occurs when Nokogiri cannot parse the provided XML content due to malformed XML, encoding issues, or invalid document structure.

Common Causes

  • Malformed XML (unclosed tags, mismatched tags)
  • Invalid characters in XML
  • Encoding mismatch
  • Namespace issues

How to Fix

# WRONG: Parsing malformed XML
xml = "<root><item>hello</root>" # Unclosed tag
doc = Nokogiri::XML(xml) # XML::SyntaxError

# CORRECT: Ensure valid XML
xml = "<root><item>hello</item></root>"
doc = Nokogiri::XML(xml)

# WRONG: Not handling encoding
xml = "\xFF\xFE<hello>" # Invalid UTF-8
doc = Nokogiri::XML(xml)

# CORRECT: Fix encoding
xml = "<hello>".encode("UTF-8", invalid: :replace, undef: :replace)
doc = Nokogiri::XML(xml)

# WRONG: Strict parsing of imperfect XML
doc = Nokogiri::XML(xml) { |config| config.strict }

# CORRECT: Lenient parsing
doc = Nokogiri::XML(xml) do |config|
 config.noerror # Suppress errors
end

Examples

# Example 1: Parse with error handling
begin
 doc = Nokogiri::XML(xml_content)
rescue Nokogiri::XML::SyntaxError => e
 puts "Parse error: #{e.message}"
end

# Example 2: Parse HTML (more lenient)
doc = Nokogiri::HTML(html_content)

# Example 3: Validate XML
errors = Nokogiri::XML(xml_content).errors
puts errors unless errors.empty?

Related Errors

  • Nokogiri XPath error — XPath query failed
  • LoadError — cannot load such file
  • Encoding error — encoding issues

item.title: [Solution] Ruby SyntaxError — Unexpected Token Fix
item.description:
SyntaxError — Unexpected Token

A SyntaxError is raised when Ruby encounters code that violates the language’s syntax rules.

Description

Ruby’s parser checks code syntax before execution. SyntaxError indicates the parser found something it can’t understand. These errors are caught at parse time, before any code runs.

Common causes:

  • Missing end keyword — unclosed blocks or methods
  • Unmatched parentheses — missing closing brackets
  • Invalid character — unexpected symbols in code
  • String interpolation issues — malformed #{} blocks

Common Causes

# Cause 1: Missing end keyword
def greet(name)
 puts "Hello, #{name}"
# SyntaxError: unexpected end-of-input

# Cause 2: Unmatched parentheses
result = (1 + 2 # SyntaxError: unexpected syntax error

# Cause 3: Invalid character
price = $100 # SyntaxError: unexpected constant

# Cause 4: String interpolation issues
str = "Hello #{name" # SyntaxError: syntax error

How to Fix

Fix 1: Add missing end keywords

# Wrong
def greet(name)
 puts "Hello, #{name}"

# Correct
def greet(name)
 puts "Hello, #{name}"
end

Fix 2: Match parentheses

# Wrong
result = (1 + 2

# Correct
result = (1 + 2)

Fix 3: Fix string interpolation

# Wrong
str = "Hello #{name"

# Correct
str = "Hello #{name}"

Fix 4: Use proper variable naming

# Wrong
price = $100 # SyntaxError

# Correct
price = 100

Examples

# Example 1: Common syntax patterns
if condition
 do_something
else
 do_other
end

# Example 2: Proper block syntax
[1, 2, 3].each { |n| puts n }
[1, 2, 3].each do |n|
 puts n
end

Related Errors

  • LoadError — file not found
  • NameError — undefined variable or constant
  • NoMethodError — undefined method for object

item.title: [Solution] SQL Syntax Error Fix
item.description:
This error occurs when the SQL parser encounters a syntax error in the statement. The message reads: You have an error in your SQL syntax; check the manual that corresponds to your MySQL server version.

What This Error Means

The SQL statement has a grammatical error that prevents the parser from understanding it. This can be caused by missing keywords, misplaced commas, unmatched quotes, or incorrect clause ordering.

Common Causes

  • Missing comma between SELECT columns
  • Unmatched quotes or parentheses
  • Reserved keyword used as column name without backticks
  • Incorrect clause ordering (WHERE before FROM)
  • Missing closing parenthesis in subquery

How to Fix

Fix 1: Check for common syntax mistakes

-- Wrong: missing comma
SELECT name email FROM users;

-- Correct
SELECT name, email FROM users;

Fix 2: Quote reserved keywords

-- Wrong: "order" is a reserved keyword
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE order = 1;

-- Correct: use backticks
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE `order` = 1;

Fix 3: Match parentheses

-- Wrong: unclosed parenthesis
SELECT * FROM (SELECT id FROM users;

-- Correct
SELECT * FROM (SELECT id FROM users) AS sub;

Fix 4: Check subquery syntax

-- Wrong: subquery must have alias
SELECT * FROM (SELECT id, name FROM users);

-- Correct
SELECT * FROM (SELECT id, name FROM users) AS user_list;

Examples

SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = 'Alice' AND;
-- ERROR 1064: You have an error in your SQL syntax near 'AND'

Related Errors

  • Unknown Column — column doesn’t exist
  • Table Not Found — table doesn’t exist

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1005 — Semicolon Expected
item.description:
TS1005 — ‘;’ expected

TS1005 is a parse error indicating TypeScript encountered a token where it expected a semicolon. This usually means there’s a syntax issue with how statements, declarations, or expressions are structured.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing semicolon after variable declaration
let x = 10
let y = 20 // TS1005 on this line — expected ';' after declaration

// Cause 2: Missing semicolon in for loop
for (let i = 0; i < 10; i++)
{ // TS1005 — expected ';' after for statement

// Cause 3: Missing semicolon after interface property
interface User {
 name: string
 age: number // TS1005 if strict semicolon enforcement
}

// Cause 4: Missing semicolon in import statement
import { helper } from "./utils"
import { other } from "./other" // TS1005

Solutions

Fix 1: Add missing semicolons

// Wrong
let x = 10
let y = 20

// Correct
let x = 10;
let y = 20;

Fix 2: Use a linter to enforce semicolons

// .eslintrc.json
{
 "rules": {
 "semi": ["error", "always"]
 }
}

Fix 3: Check for missing closing brackets or braces

// Wrong — missing closing parenthesis
const result = add(1, 2
// TS1005: expected ';' — really means missing ')'

// Correct
const result = add(1, 2);

Fix 4: Fix malformed expressions

// Wrong — double operator
let x = 10 ++ 5; // TS1005

// Correct
let x = 10 + 5;

Examples

// Example 1: Accidentally using a keyword
let class = "myClass"; // TS1005: ';' expected (class is reserved)

// Example 2: Forgetting semicolon in chained method
const result = items
 .filter(x => x.active)
 .map(x => x.name) // OK with ASI
 .join(", "); // TS1005 if context requires semicolon

// Fix: add semicolon at end
const result = items
 .filter(x => x.active)
 .map(x => x.name)
 .join(", ");

Related Errors

  • TS1109: Expression expected — related parse error.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — broader parse error.
  • TS1434: Unexpected keyword or identifier — unexpected token.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1109 — Expression Expected
item.description:
TS1109 — Expression expected

TS1109 is a parse error indicating TypeScript encountered a token where it expected an expression. This usually happens with malformed function calls, missing operands, or incomplete code.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Empty function call argument
const result = Math.random() // Missing closing paren or argument

// Cause 2: Missing right operand
let x = 5 + ; // TS1109: expression expected after '+'

// Cause 3: Incomplete arrow function
const fn = () => // TS1109: expression expected after '=>'

// Cause 4: Missing value in assignment
const obj = {
 key: // TS1109: expression expected after ':'
};

Solutions

Fix 1: Complete the expression

// Wrong
let x = 5 + ;

// Correct
let x = 5 + 10;

Fix 2: Close parentheses and provide arguments

// Wrong
const result = Math.random()

// Correct
const result = Math.random();

Fix 3: Provide a return value for arrow functions

// Wrong
const fn = () =>

// Correct
const fn = () => 42;

// Or with block body
const fn = () => {
 return 42;
};

Fix 4: Complete object property values

// Wrong
const obj = {
 key:
};

// Correct
const obj = {
 key: "value"
};

Examples

// Example 1: Incomplete template literal
const name = `Hello, ${; // TS1109

// Fix:
const name = `Hello, ${user.name}`;

// Example 2: Missing argument in new expression
const el = new HTMLElement(); // If HTMLElement isn't defined
// TS1109 if the expression can't be resolved

Related Errors

  • TS1005: Semicolon expected — missing semicolons.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — broader token error.
  • TS1434: Unexpected keyword or identifier — unexpected tokens.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1128 — Declaration or Statement Expected
item.description:
TS1128 — Declaration or statement expected

TS1128 occurs when TypeScript encounters a token where it expects a declaration (variable, function, class, etc.) or a statement. This often indicates a structural syntax problem like extra closing braces, misplaced code, or invalid top-level constructs.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Extra closing brace
function greet() {
 console.log("hello");
}} // TS1128: extra '}' — declaration or statement expected

// Cause 2: Code outside of a function or class at invalid location
function process() {}
 const x = 10; // TS1128: unexpected indentation / misplaced code

// Cause 3: Missing function body or expression
function compute() // TS1128: expected '{' or '=>'

// Cause 4: Invalid top-level expression
42 // TS1128 if not in a module or script context properly

Solutions

Fix 1: Remove extra closing braces

// Wrong
function greet() {
 console.log("hello");
}}

// Correct
function greet() {
 console.log("hello");
}

Fix 2: Ensure code is inside proper blocks

// Wrong
function process() {}
 const x = 10; // outside the function

// Correct
function process() {
 const x = 10; // inside the function
}

Fix 3: Complete function declarations

// Wrong
function compute()

// Correct
function compute(): number {
 return 42;
}

Fix 4: Check for misplaced imports or exports

// Wrong — export outside module context
export default {} // TS1128 if file is not a module

// Correct — ensure the file has at least one import/export
import { something } from "./other";
export default {};

Examples

// Example 1: Accidentally closing a block early
if (condition) {
 doSomething();
} // Extra close
doOtherThing(); // TS1128

// Example 2: Mixing declaration and expression
const obj = {
 method() {}
}
const x = 10 // Missing semicolon, may cause TS1128 on next line

Related Errors

  • TS1005: Semicolon expected — missing semicolons.
  • TS1109: Expression expected — incomplete expressions.
  • TS1434: Unexpected keyword or identifier — unexpected tokens.

item.title: PHP Parse error: syntax error, unexpected token
item.description:
PHP Parse error: syntax error, unexpected token

A parse error occurs when the PHP parser encounters code that violates the language grammar. PHP cannot execute any code in a file that has a parse error — it stops immediately and reports the file, line number, and problematic token.

Common Causes

  • Missing semicolons at the end of statements
  • Unclosed strings, brackets, or parentheses
  • Using old-style array syntax incorrectly
  • Mismatched quotes or unescaped characters inside strings

How to Fix

Add Missing Semicolons

<?php
// Wrong
$greeting = "Hello"
echo $greeting

// Correct
$greeting = "Hello";
echo $greeting;
?>

Close All Brackets and Parentheses

<?php
// Wrong — missing closing parenthesis
function greet($name) {
 echo "Hello " . $name;
}

// Correct
function greet($name) {
 echo "Hello " . $name;
}
?>

Escape Quotes Inside Strings

<?php
// Wrong
echo 'It's broken';

// Correct
echo 'It\'s fixed';
echo "It's fixed";
?>

Examples

<?php
// This triggers a parse error
$arr = array(1, 2, 3)
echo $arr[0]
// Parse error: syntax error, unexpected token ","
?>

Use php -l to lint-check files without executing them:
